News.title: Moment drunk yob HEADBUTTS Wetherspoons manager as she kicks him out over phone charger row
News.content:
THIS is the shocking moment a drunken yob headbutted the manager of a Wetherspoons after being told to leave.

Leon Wimsett, 24, assaulted the female victim – who is not being named – at The Peter Cushing pub in Whitstable, Kent.





A court heard the groundworker was convinced he’d left his phone charging behind the bar – despite staff telling him they don’t do this for customers.

Wimsett then “lost his temper” and delivered a vicious headbutt, having gotten into another fight in the pub earlier in the evening.

Footage shows him arguing with the manager of the pub as she calmly asks him to leave and points towards the exit on December 19 last year.

The thug then suddenly assaulted her without warning as shocked bystanders watched on.

Wimsett, of Herne Bay, Kent, admitted charges of assault and being drunk and disorderly but was spared jail and handed fines and costs totalling £739.

Mark Wickham, chair of the bench, said the footage of the incident had shocked both him and his colleagues.

He handed Wimsett a suspended prison sentence for the assault, and also ordered him to pay £739 in costs and fines, including £500 compensation to the manager of the Wetherspoons.

He was also given a restraining order, banning him from visiting the pub, while no separate penalty was given for a second charge of being drunk and disorderly.



Prosecutor Neil Sweeney read a statement from the pub manager to Margate magistrates’ court: “At around 10:45pm, there had been a fight. Included in this group was the man who later assaulted me.

“He complained his phone was on charge behind the bar, and we were keeping it from him. I explained we hadn’t done that.

“[My colleague] went to the end of the bar. [Wimsett] refused to talk to me.

“None of what he was saying made any sense. I told him he needed to leave and that we didn’t have his phone.

“He kept saying he would call the police. He then headbutted me, hitting me in the nose.



“As a result, I have fallen back into staff. [Wimsett] then left. The whole incident lasted about ten minutes.”

The court heard police were called, with Wimsett later identified as the attacker by witnesses at the scene.

Mr Sweeney added: “The [manager’s] statement does not do justice to the severity of the assault.

“[Wimsett] is under the influence of alcohol. It’s not just a headbutt, it’s a real lunge.

“It’s a proper headbutt to a female working in a pub. It did make connection with her nose.



“There is use of substantial force. At the end, you can hear her whimpering or crying.

“It could have been a far more serious injury.”

Mr Sweeney added that Wimsett, who lives with his parents, had a “proven history of violence”, citing two previous “like matters” of common assault.

The court heard Wimsett had taken cocaine as well as drinking on the night of the attack, and was “embarrassed” and “ashamed” by what he’d done.

Roger Davies, defending, said: “I don’t think he’s seen the video.”I don’t think he remembers what happened.



“But I described it to him and he winced. He’s embarrassed and ashamed.

“[Wimsett] does not want these events in his life. He recognises his responsibility and his shame.”

Mr Wickham told Wimsett: “It’s totally unacceptable and really quite shocking for all of us to see.

“We feel it has passed the custody threshold. It was committed whilst you were on a community order.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“It was also against a person providing a public duty and you were under the influence of alcohol at the time.”



As he left the dock, Wimsett thanked the bench for sparing him jail, saying: “You won’t see me again, I promise you.”

News.title: Cruel conwoman sat on frail pensioner’s lap to ‘woo’ him into handing over £20k in twisted family
News.content:
SICK SCHEME Cruel conwoman sat on frail pensioner’s lap to ‘woo’ him into handing over £20k in twisted family

A CONWOMAN sat on a pensioner’s lap and tried to “woo” him into handing over £20,000 to her fraudster family weeks before he died.

Charlotte Cassidy, 29, targeted Paul Lambert and tried to steal from his pension helped by her brother Michael Cassidy, 21, and father William Cassidy, 53.





The trio were employed to carry out work on Mr Lambert’s home while Charlotte Cassidy befriended the lonely grandfather and would sit on his lap rubbing cream into his leg.

These cruel attempts to “woo” Mr Lambert were all so he would buy her a Rolex watch.

Charlotte Cassidy drugged the frail pensioner with a sleeping pill, searched through his personal files and sat in on a meeting with his financial advisor.

The Cassidy family charged Mr Lambert £18,000 for “shoddy” work on his home while they tried to exploit more money from him.



Charlotte Cassidy even impersonated the elderly man and tried to take £15,000 from his pension, but fortunately the financial advisor spotted the missing funds.

Mr Lambert refused to believe she was behind the attempted theft and was left “heartbroken” when he heard a recording of Charlotte Cassidy’s call to obtain the money.

Claire Taylor, Mr Lambert’s daughter, said: “He couldn’t understand how anyone could do that, especially someone he believed was a friend.”

Claire said her father was a “hard-working, family man” who had battled mental health problems and alcohol addiction in his old age.



She added: “He was extremely lonely and struggling with living alone. We did our best to support him but it was extremely difficult with work and having young families.”

Charlotte Cassidy befriended Claire’s dad and would take him and make him pay for everything she said.

The conwoman would rub cream into his legs, sit in on private appointments, take Mr Lambert to buy a rolex or to the bank to withdraw money, Claire continued.

She said: “He was heartbroken, mainly deteriorated and was found dead a few weeks later. His house was unfinished and looked like a building site and we paid £6,000 to put work right after his death. The work carried out by the Cassidy family was valued at nothing.”

The family were sentenced at Cardiff Crown Court and prosecutor Ian Ibrahim said they conned Mr Lambert out of £20,900.



The court previously heard how the Cassidy family ran a business called Fix A Drive which conned other victims and intimated them for “payments” after doing a terrible job.

When one couple refused to pay, William Cassidy told them they were from the Traveller community and he would return to rip up their driveway with an excavator.

The victims were left £33,700 out of pocket, the court heard.

Charlotte Cassidy, of Hardwicke, Gloucestershire, admitted to four counts under the Fraud Act and was given an 18-month suspended sentence.

She will wear an electronic tag for four months, carry out 100 hours of unpaid work and participate in a 20 day rehabilitation activity programme.



Despite taking £71,709 in her offending, Charlotte Cassidy only had £7,904 of assets, a Proceeds of Crime Act hearing at Merthyr Tydfil Crown Court heard.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Judge Eugene Egan ordered her to pay the £7,904 within three months or face three months imprisonment in default.

Michael Cassidy had no available assets despite taking more than £38,000 from the schemes, and will pay a nominal sum of £10 in three months.

News.title: Iran war chaos sees travel advice changed for 10 countries - full list
News.content:
Iran war chaos sees travel advice changed for 10 countries - full list

The Foreign Office has issued new travel advice for a string of countries in the Middle East and further afield amid the escalating conflict between Iran and the USA.

View 4 Images The Foreign Office has updated its travel advice as war spreads across the Middle East (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

The Foreign Office has updated its travel advice for British tourists heading to 10 destinations as war continues to spread across the Middle East.

‌



Iranian drones been fired at countries across the Gulf in response to the US-Israeli shelling of the country, sparking widespread airspace closures that have plunged aviation into its biggest crisis since the Covid pandemic.

‌



Thousands of British nationals remain stranded abroad as the government draws up plans for evacuations, with those currently in the Middle East told to stay indoors and keep up to date with information from local authorities. In the past 24 hours, the Foreign Office has amended travel guidance for Thailand, Egypt, Jordan, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Pakistan, Israel, Bahrain and Kuwait. Here's the current advice for each country:

‌



View 4 Images Air travel around the world has been impacted by airspace closures (Image: Bloomberg via Getty Images)

Thailand

Escalation in the Middle East has caused widespread travel disruption, including airspace closures, delayed and cancelled flights.

Your travel plans may be affected, even if your destination is not in the Middle East.

‌



UAE

The Foreign Office currently advises against all but essential travel to United Arab Emirates, including Dubai and Abu Dhabi.

There are a limited number of commercial options available, including by air from UAE and from Oman. If your presence in UAE is not essential, you may wish to consider departing.

View 4 Images Dubai has come under fire from Iranian drones (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



Saudi Arabia

If you judge it is safe to do so and plan to use commercial options to depart check for the latest updates from your airline or tour operator, as well as the instructions of local authorities and the status of any border crossings before you travel.

Make sure you have access to emergency supplies and essential medication. Travel within or out of Saudi Arabia is at your own risk.

Qatar

The Foreign Office says limited flights are now operating in and out of Qatar, and that British holidaymakers should leave if it is safe to do so.

‌



Egypt

International borders in Israel and Palestine could close at short notice, including the Taba border crossing between Israel and Egypt.

The Foreign Office said: "Check with local authorities and read Israel travel advice and Palestine travel advice before trying to cross."

Kuwait

According to the Foreign Office, if you judge it is safe to leave Kuwait, the following land border crossings into Saudi Arabia are currently open as of yesterday:

‌



Al Salmi / Ar – Ruqi

Al Nuwaisib / Al -Khafji

The advice adds: " If travelling by car, the vehicle must be wholly owned and registered in your name.

"If you plan to use commercial options to depart check for the latest updates from your airline or tour operator, as well as the instructions of local authorities and the status of any border crossings before you travel.

‌



"Make sure you have access to emergency supplies and essential medication. Travel within or out of Kuwait is at your own risk.

"Keep your departure plans under review and ensure your travel documents are up to date, including any visas required for onward travel."

Bahrain

The Foreign Office said: "If you judge it is safe to do so and you plan to travel by road read our Travel Advice for Saudi Arabia for entry requirements to Saudi Arabia and ensure you have the correct paperwork, or you may be denied entry.

‌



"If staying in the Kingdom, follow the instructions of the local authorities. If you are advised to take shelter, stay indoors or find the nearest safe building or designated shelter.

"An interior stairwell or a room with as few external walls or windows as possible may provide additional protection."

View 4 Images At least 11 people have died in Israel amid Iranian rocket attacks (Image: ABIR SULTAN/EPA/Shutterstock)

‌



Israel

The Foreign Office currently advises against all travel to Israel.

The Israeli Ministry of Tourism is providing shuttle buses from Jerusalem and Tel Aviv to the Taba border (Egypt). British tourists that entered on a B2 tourist visa are eligible to register for this service. British nationals who are resident in Israel or are dual Israeli nationals are not eligible for this service.

A British Embassy team is present on the Egyptian side of the Taba border to point British Nationals to options for onward travel to Sharm El-Sheikh Airport.

Article continues below

Palestine

You should not travel to Palestine, according to the latest Foreign Office advice.

Jordan

The Foreign Office has updated information about Jordanian airspace, which has been affected by the regional conflict. The department continues to advise against all but essential travel to Jordan, and all travel to within 3km of the border with Syria.

News.title: Reports shows majority of women now happy to go makeup-free - here's why
News.content:
Reports shows majority of women now happy to go makeup-free - here's why

A major new study women has revealed the majority of women are now confident and comfortable enough in their skin they're happy to leave the house make-up free

View 2 Images Women admit they are happier with their appearance now than they were five years ago (Image: Getty Images)

Three-quarters of women now feel confident enough in their skin to venture out without makeup, thanks to a generational shift towards natural beauty, according to a major new report. Research involving 5,000 women found that 65% of Gen Z and 52% of Millennials reckon they're more at ease with their appearance than they were five years ago.

‌



Two-thirds revealed that maintaining a skincare regime has helped boost their confidence. However, this comes after 66% confessed their skin had previously taken a toll on their personal relationships, with 19% having dodged social gatherings due to a poor skin day, whilst 22% had avoided venturing out altogether.

‌



View 2 Images Seven in ten described their routine as 'simple and consistent' (Image: Getty Images)

‌



These findings emerge from a Liz Earle Beauty Co. report, which surveyed women about their relationship with their skin, examining everything from ingredient knowledge to confidence levels, routines and rituals.

Liz Earle Beauty Co. facialist and lifestyle ambassador, Sarah Carr, commented: "Our skin confidence has a big impact on how we feel generally. When we do feel good about our skin, it empowers us to be more present and engaged in everyday life."

The study revealed that 63% believe 'good skin days' can completely transform their outlook for the day ahead.

‌



The research also discovered women typically use three skincare products on average, with 70% describing their regime as 'simple and consistent'. Meanwhile, 15% admitted theirs involves numerous details and multiple stages, according to the OnePoll.com data.

When it comes to purchasing beauty products, cost (48%) emerges as the primary deciding factor, with natural ingredients (41%) and quality sourcing (30%) following closely behind.

Although scientific backing (27%), sustainability and ethics (17%) and brand heritage and reputation (13%) also influenced purchasing patterns.

‌



Still, many buyers confessed to being overwhelmed and confused when buying products for their skin. A further 41% admit they don't fully understand what various skincare ingredients do.

Meanwhile, product saturation and fast beauty burnout appeared widespread, with 78% believing there are far too many skincare launches to keep track off.

Sarah Carr from the beauty brand, added: "We're seeing a shift towards embracing natural beauty and feeling comfortable going makeup free.

Article continues below

"Taking the time to invest in a skincare routine has become a form of self-care, helping women to ultimately nurture their skin and their wellbeing.

"People clearly benefit from these routines, but they're steering away from buzz words and hundreds of 'new' products cycling onto the market, craving straightforward products with natural, quality ingredients with the scientific backing to produce the results they promise."

News.title: US and Israel pitching £1m missiles against £50,000 drones in war of attrition
News.content:
US and Israel pitching £1m missiles against £50,000 drones in war of attrition

Iran battles on as war-planners calculate their rate of fire to avoid expending too many defensive missiles costing £1 mllion each - the deadly calculation is it's not sustainable

View 5 Images Smoke billows from airstrike on Tehran (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

The US-Israeli war on Iran is reaching a critical stage as all sides carefully calculate their rate of fire in terms of missiles, bombs, drones and defensive munitions. Iran has clearly set out to drive for a war of attrition, a conflict involving endurance no matter the consequences and despite casualties that have soared past 1,000.

‌



It also wanted to inflict as much damage to the economies of nearby Gulf States in a bid to exert leverage over them and it has hit homes, hotels and industrial sites., as it has taken lives too. It also has a limited number of missiles, rockets and drones and their largely low level of sophistication means they are sometimes difficult to combat. Low flying, cumbersome and slow drones might only require something as simple as a machine gun to bring down but wasting a £1million Amraam missile on them is unsustainable.

‌



View 5 Images Israeli Apache attack helicopter on Lebanon border (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



OPINION Chris Hughes Expect a long war - new Iran leader Motjaba Khamenei is a tough hardliner

‌



These were what was used by UK Typhoons and F-35B fast jets in the past couple of days over Jordan as they tricked incoming drones and brought them down. But an attack helicopter could have brought them down using perhaps £100 worth of machine gun rounds.

And the US and Israel have a finite number of defensive missiles so they will be trying economise as the tempo of battle slows. Plus - keeping the US aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln operational in a threat of war, along with its three guided missile destroyers costs in the region of £8million a-day.

View 5 Images Shepherd boy near missile in Qamshili, Syria (Image: AP)

‌



One thing is clear - that Iran does not have the same store of missiles as it had before five days ago when the war began and it is trying to slow its rate of fire. But at the same time the US and Israel do not want a protracted war they have to step back from if they expend their supplies of defensive missiles.

Western officials told the Mirror today that Iran is expected to be able to fire missiles for several days more but there are also fears that may be an underestimate. It is possible they could last for weeks. And even then the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps will resort to ‘asymmetric warfare’ of terror attacks in the region if it is able - and there are significant warnings it will.

View 5 Images Cost of war: Huge damage in Tehran (Image: Getty Images)

‌



And it is just possible Iran hopes it can outlast the rate at which Israel and the US plus other allies expend their interceptor missiles. This will, the regime hopes, force the US and Israel to stop the war, whilst all parties may be able to claim some sort of victory - Trump because he inflicted casualties and changed the Iranian regime, whilst hitting its ballistic and nuclear sites and Netanyahu because he can claim a pre-emptive strike on Iran.

Iran, however will likely claim simply that it stood up to the “Great Satan” as it calls America and at the same time attacked Israel. All of them are of course hollow and pointless victories, immensely costly in terms of human life, the global economy and world stability. Former Israeli military intelligence operative Danny Citrinowicz told the Mirror: “Iran started the war with between 2,000 and 3,000 medium range missiles, more than 5,000 short range missiles and I think between eight and 10,000 drones.

View 5 Images US President Donald Trump (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

Article continues below

“But we don’t know how many launchers they have. They have to calculate how many they use as they want a war of attrition and have to regulate their use. They have to make sure they’re not going to exhaust their arsenal. I believe they are planning for a war of a couple of weeks, perhaps even more than that. They have been damaged but they still have the capacity to launch barrages towards the State of Israel and to use drones on the Gulf States.

“I believe the next level of escalation will be to attack the energy facilities in those countries - and I believe they hope we will run out of interceptor missiles. If we see that depleting in such a way that becomes harmful then maybe then Israel will end the war.”

News.title: NHS tells millions in UK '10 minute test' could 'save your life' - how to book
News.content:
NHS tells millions in UK '10 minute test' could 'save your life' - how to book

People are being urged to come forward for the potentially life-saving test

View 2 Images The health test is free on the NHS (stock photo) (Image: Getty)

Millions of people in the UK are being urged to complete an NHS test that takes around ten minutes. As NHS London explains, the test could save lives. Posting on X as @NHSEnglandLDN, the health service urged people to come forward for cervical screening.

‌



The post said: "Cervical screening checks for high-risk types of the HPV virus. It is the best way to find out who is at higher risk of developing the cell changes that could lead to cervical cancer."

‌



NHS London added: "Cervical screening typically takes around 10 minutes and could save your life." The X post also linked to the NHS website, which shares more information about the test, sometimes known as a smear test.

‌



All women aged 25 to 64 are invited for cervical screening every five years, and everyone with a cervix should be offered screening. Millions of people in the UK are eligible.

Screening does not test for cancer, but it's a test to help prevent cancer. The NHS explains: "During the screening appointment, a small sample of cells will be taken from your cervix. The sample is checked for certain types of human papillomavirus (HPV) that can cause changes to the cells of your cervix. These are called high-risk types of HPV.

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



"If these types of HPV are not found, you do not need any further tests. If these types of HPV are found, the sample is checked for any changes in the cells of your cervix. These can be treated to help prevent cervical cancer."

While an appointment typically lasts around ten minutes, the screening test should take less than five minutes, according to the NHS. The health service's website shares a step-by-step breakdown explaining what to expect during screening, and a video people can watch.

How can you book a cervical screening?

The NHS says: "You'll be sent an invitation through the NHS App, by text or by letter when it's time to book your cervical screening appointment. Your invitation will tell you where you can go for cervical screening and how to book.

‌



"Most cervical screening is done in a GP surgery by a female nurse or doctor. You can usually call your GP surgery to book an appointment with them. You might be able to book the appointment online. Find your GP surgery In some parts of England, you may be able to go to a local sexual health clinic or walk-in centre instead."

View 2 Images The appointment only takes around ten minutes (stock photo) (Image: Getty)

According to Cancer Research UK, 99.8% of cervical cancer cases are preventable. Anyone with a cervix can get cervical cancer, but it is most common in women aged between 30 and 35. The NHS says symptoms of cervical cancer include:

Article continues below

vaginal bleeding that's unusual for you – including bleeding during or after sex, between your periods or after the menopause, or having heavier periods than usual

changes to your vaginal discharge

pain during sex

pain in your lower back, between your hip bones (pelvis), or in your lower tummy

See a GP if you have any health concerns, such as if you have any symptoms of cervical cancer. The NHS explained: "These symptoms are very common and can be caused by many different conditions.

"Having them does not definitely mean you have cervical cancer. But it's important to get them checked by a GP. This is because if they're caused by cancer, finding it early means treatment is more likely to be successful."

News.title: Moment Brit thrown off plane for 'vaping and slapping flight attendant's bum'
News.content:
Moment Brit thrown off plane for 'vaping and slapping flight attendant's bum'

A 58-year-old-man was detained by police in Taiwan after he allegedly slapped a flight attendant's bum, vaped and swore at other passengers on a 13-hour fight from London

British passenger thrown off China Airlines plane for unruly behaviour

Video footage has captured the moment a British traveller was kicked off a plane after he allegedly slapped a flight attendant's bum and vaped on the chaotic flight.

‌



The 58-year-old man, who appeared to be wearing a Man Utd jacket, was reportedly disruptive throughout the 13-hour China Airlines flight from London. His supposed actions included shouting and swearing at other passengers, on his way to Taiwan.

‌



The Brit was then seen smoking an e-cigarette in his seat, according to witnesses. The man supposedly also touched the bums of both male and female flight attendants, before allegedly slapping a female attendant's behind with force.

‌



View 4 Images A British man wearing a Man United jacket was hauled off a plane (Image: JamPress)

Flight attendant urges all travellers to stop wearing black socks on planes READ MORE :

The passenger was also accused of raising the flight crew's hands, while talking to them, before planting a kiss on them. The Brit was taken into custody after arriving at Taoyuan International Airport in Taiwan on Tuesday, March 3.

Shocking footage caught the moment uniformed officers dragged the man off the plane while he appeared to apologise. This passenger supposedly said: "I apologise, I'm sorry. I touched your bum, f*** sake." He appeared to add: "What is she, a paedophile?"

‌



View 4 Images The Brit allegedly vaped and slapped a flight attendant's bum (Image: JamPress)

During the man's alleged antics on the flight, his wife reportedly defended him by claiming "he likes to joke", sparking outrage among the other passengers. They told her "it's not funny" and "you're an accomplice".

One passenger told him: "You're in Taiwan now, not England." His wife then asked him to stop, saying: "Everyone says you're being noisy." Upon arrival at the airport, the China Airlines cabin crew informed the aviation police and the British passenger was taken into custody.

‌



View 4 Images The man was taken into custody when the flight landed in Taiwan (Image: JamPress)

The man reportedly faces a fine for vaping on the plane. He reportedly could face further charges of harassment or public indecency if any flight attendants decide to file a complaint.

A China Airlines spokesperson emphasised that they have a zero-tolerance policy for unruly behaviour. They pointed out that it is strictly illegal to bring e-cigarettes, vapes and similar products into Taiwan. The airline said they will fully cooperate with any legal proceedings.

‌



View 4 Images The Brit man's wife allegedly said 'he likes to joke' (Image: JamPress)

This comes after two Jet2 passengers were kicked off a flight and banned from the airline after a shocking fight broke out mid-air. Footage from the plane, from Antalya, Turkey, to Manchester, suggested several travellers became entangled in the chaos.

One video seemingly captures three men in the aircraft's aisle, with one lunging to snatch a person's phone before attempting to grab the face of a person ahead of him. The passenger at the heart of the commotion seems to be shoved backwards, whilst another man tries to calm the situation.

Article continues below

A passenger further away seemed to then start gesturing towards someone else. That individual then seemingly pushes the initial man involved in the disturbance before reaching out to grab him. At one point a passenger is seen in a headlock. A spokesperson for Jet2 said: "We can confirm that the two disruptive passengers will be banned from flying with us for life, and we will also vigorously pursue them to recover the costs that we incurred as a result of this diversion."

News.title: Andy Burnham says by-election loss shows 'depth of the chasm between people and Westminster'
News.content:
Andy Burnham says by-election loss shows 'depth of the chasm between people and Westminster'

The Greater Manchester Mayor made the comments less than a week on from the Greens Hannah Spencer storming to victory in Gorton and Denton, with 14,980 votes - a majority of 4,402

View 2 Images Andy Burnham had hoped to stand in Gorton and Denton (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

Andy Burnham claimed the Gorton and Denton by-election loss “revealed the full depth of the chasm between people and Westminster politics”.

‌



In his first comments since the defeat, the Greater Manchester Mayor called for a “new political culture” and warned people no longer trust politicians to fix the basics. His intervention comes less than a week on from the Greens Hannah Spencer storming to victory in the Greater Manchester seat, with 14,980 votes - a majority of 4,402.

‌



Speaking at a Centre For Cities event, Mr Burnham also highlighted the failure to pay social care staff properly, something he argued contributed to the NHS being in "permanent crisis”.

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

View 2 Images Labour Party candidate Angeliki Stogia was joined by Mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham and Deputy Labour leader Lucy Powell during the campaign (Image: PA)

He said: “From my point of view, I’m definitely not here to talk about my career plans, other than to say I wrote a book as you might know, with the mayor of the Liverpool City region Steve Rotheram about leaving Westminster and establishing devolution across England.

‌



“It’s called ‘Head North’. All I can say today is that the sequel ‘Head South’ is currently on hold. What I want to say today is that the time has most definitely come for a serious conversation about our political system and its pervading culture, particularly so in the aftermath of the Gorton and Denton by-election. It revealed the full depth of the chasm between people and Westminster politics. I don’t think anybody can seriously dispute that statement.”

Mr Burnham, who was blocked by Labour's ruling body from standing in the contest, told the event a failure to fix social care had contributed towards people losing faith in Westminster. He said: “It's unbelievable how people on the frontline of social care do not get a basic enough salary to support themselves, even though they are doing incredible things to look after people. So no wonder is it that the NHS is in permanent permanent crisis, winter crisis because the preventative end just cannot cope.

"Basically, Westminster, it’s just almost like they said ‘well, we'll just leave that.’ It's just an incredible state of affairs to be honest. So if politics is once again to regain people's trust and be about long term problem solving, I think we have to build a new political culture entirely.”

Article continues below

Mr Burnham claimed the public simply didn’t trust politicians to fix their basic needs, using the example of potholes. He explained: “Is there anything that more explains the levels of alienation people feel politics can't fix a pothole?

“The collapse of local government and the failure of Westminster to fix the financing of local government to me, that is a really big thing that underlies the alienated political climate that we experience today. People just don't see politics as able to fix the basics.”

In a further attack on Westminster, Mr Burnham argued it was not about addressing problems, but point scoring. An MP for 16 years, he said: “I just saw the political, if you like, culture just becoming more and more shallow. It simply doesn't try to fix the big problems, the aim is to score points across the house, isn't it? It's not there to fix things.”

News.title: CIA 'trying to arm Kurdish forces in Iran in attempt to spark uprising'
News.content:
CIA 'trying to arm Kurdish forces in Iran in attempt to spark uprising'

Kurdish forces have allegedly been in contact with Donald Trump about launching an uprising in Iran amid an ongoing bombing campaign by the US and Israel in the country

View 3 Images Kurds have reportedly been in contact with US officials (Image: Getty Images)

The CIA is reportedly trying to arm Kurdish forces within Iran in a bid to spark an uprising against the remnants of the regime in Tehran.

‌



Donald Trump's administration is understood to have been in active communication with minority groups within Iran, including Kurdish leaders in neighbouring Iraq, that could lead to them being given military support. Iranian Kurds have forces along the Iraq-Iran border and have suggested they could launch an operation with the goal being greater autonomy or the creation of an independent state.

‌



Iran's Revolutionary Guard has started bombing campaigns against Kurds in a bid to prevent an attack. Trump is said to have spoken with the president of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan, a senior Kurdish official said.

‌



View 3 Images Many Kurds want an independent homeland (Image: Getty Images)

Brit stranded in Dubai with rare cancer 'terrified' health deteriorating READ MORE :

Iranian Kurdish forces are set to commence a ground offensive in Western Iran in the next few days. A source told CNN: "We believe we have a big chance now." They added militias expect US and Israeli support.

Trump is also said to have called Iraqi Kurdish leaders over the weekend to discuss the US military operation in Iran and how they could work with the Kurds as the mission moves forward, according to two US officials and a third source familiar with the conversations. But any attempt to arm Kurdish militias in Iran would require the support of their counterparts in Iraq. They would use Iraqi Kurdistan as a hub to launch operations from.

‌



One person familiar with the discussions said t he idea would be for Kurdish armed forces to take on Iranian security forces and pin them down. This would be done to then make it easier for unarmed Iranians in big cities to take to the streets with less risk of the massacres that were seen in the region in January.

Another US official told the network that Kurds could spark chaos in the region and force a conflict with the Iranian regime. They hope the move would leave the Revolutionary Guard spread too thin.

View 3 Images Kurds are battle hardened after multiple conflicts against ISIS, Turkey and Syrian forces (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

‌



But a wider uprising in Iran would prove difficult as much of the population is unarmed. Alex Plitsas, a senior Pentagon official under Barack Obama, said the US is "clearly trying to jump-start" an uprising.

He added: "The Iranian people are generally unarmed as a whole and unless the security services collapse, it’ll be difficult for them to take over unless someone arms them. I believe the US is hopeful that this will inspire others on the ground in Iran to do the same."

It is unclear whether any uprising would throw Iran into greater turmoil and unclear whether an independent Kurdish state would be accepted by a new Iranian regime or other nations in the Middle East. Many countries, such as Iraq, Syria and Turkey have Kurdish separatist movements.

Article continues below

Several of the groups have released public statements since the beginning of the war, hinting that action could be imminent and calling on Iranian military forces to defect.

News.title: He confessed to killing a 3-year-old girl – 55 years later he still hasn't been prosecuted
News.content:
He confessed to killing a 3-year-old girl – 55 years later he still hasn't been prosecuted

Police investigating the unsolved disapparance of a Bristol-born child discovered a bombshell confession that could have provided a solution to the decades-long mystery

View 6 Images Three-year-old Cheryl Grimmer, originally from Bristol, vanished from a shower block in Wollongong, New South Wales, on 12 January 1970 (Image: Daily Mirror)

On a warm January day in 1970, Carole Grimmer took her four young children to Fairy Meadow Beach in Wollongong, a resort city just over an hour’s drive south of Sydney.

‌



Carole, who had emigrated to Australia from Knowle, near Bristol, two years earlier, had left her oldest child, Ricki, in charge of his siblings. As the family outing drew to a close, Ricki — who was seven at the time — took his brothers Stephen and Paul, along with their little sister Cheryl, to rinse sand off at the beach’s shower block.

‌



While Ricki, Stephen and Paul quickly finished and returned to their mother, three-year-old Cheryl mischievously refused to leave the shower block. Tragically, she was never seen again.

‌



Although a 17-year-old suspect later confessed to killing Cheryl, no one has ever stood trial for her murder. The suspect, who has never been officially named and is referred to in court documents as “Mercury,” told police: “Some of the children started to walk away and this little girl hung back. I came around from the back of the shower block and grabbed the little girl.”

“I guess I must have strangled her,” he said, adding, “I did that to the little girl; I didn’t mean to do it.”

View 6 Images The Grimmer family had been enjoying a day at the beach (Image: PA)

‌



In his written confession, “Mercury” added: “I tied a handkerchief and a shoelace around her mouth to stop her screaming and with the other shoelace I tied up her hands”.

“There was some bloke sitting on the wall in front of the pavilion,” he said, “so I had to put my hand over her mouth to stop her screaming. Because if she had of screamed [ sic ], he would have heard it.”

He said he had wanted to take Cheryl’s towel and distinctive royal blue swimming costume home as trophies, but explained: “I decided not to because I thought my mother might find them, so I burnt them in an incinerator on a beach.”

‌



View 6 Images Cheryl had playfully refused to do what her brother told her (Image: PA)

Within hours of Cheryl’s disappearance, around 1,000 people had gathered to search the area around the beach for a missing child described as “height 3 feet 9 inches, small build, blonde short hair cut square around the back of the head, full fringe in front, fair complexion and blue eyes.”

Within days, police announced that two witnesses had come forward. They claimed to have seen a “swarthy, small man” wearing a floppy hat carrying a small girl under his arm. At around the same time, a ransom note was sent to police demanding 10,000 Australian dollars for Cheryl’s return.

‌



The note, which experts said had probably been written by a teenager, was dismissed as a hoax. After months passed with no further evidence emerging, the extensive police operation was scaled down.

View 6 Images Cheryl Grimmer with her father Vince (Image: SWNS)

Fifteen months after Cheryl’s disappearance, “Mercury” came forward to confess that he had killed her. Police noted that he had previously made a false confession to an unrelated murder, had a history of absconding from detention centres, and was known to have drug problems.

‌



Prosecutors elected not to charge him, and Cheryl’s disappearance remained unsolved.

Her body was never found, and it was not until 2011 that an inquest was held. The coroner formally ruled that Cheryl had died shortly after her disappearance, recording an open verdict on the “manner and cause” of death, and recommended that police reopen the investigation.

At the inquest, Carole Grimmer said she believed her daughter was still alive. The confession was barely mentioned. “She was old and frail at the time and quite ill,” Ricki told CNN. “I don’t think it totally computed with her, because it was rushed over so fast.”

‌



View 6 Images Cheryl was officially declared dead in 2011, when she would have been 45 (Image: SWNS)

New South Wales Police Detective Sergeant Damian Loone and Senior Constable Frank Sanvitale were tasked with re-examining the evidence.

After reviewing the confession, DS Loone said: “It was explosive… I was at a loss as to why he hadn’t been charged.”

‌



Original investigators had believed they could match “Mercury’s” description of the crime scene to the area around Fairy Meadow Beach, but later examination showed that assessment had been mistaken.

Consequently, in March 2017, “Mercury,” then 63, was arrested and extradited to New South Wales to face a charge of murdering Cheryl.

View 6 Images Cheryl's parents went to their graves not knowing about the confession (Image: Daily Mirror)

Article continues below

However, legal technicalities prevented his prosecution. Justice Robert Hulme ruled that “Mercury’s” initial confession was inadmissible because, at 17, he should have been accompanied by a responsible adult.

His name could not be reported, but in October last year New South Wales Legislative Council member Jeremy Buckingham used parliamentary privilege to reveal the suspect’s real name and details of his confession.

In February, Mr Buckingham returned to parliament to reveal the most recent name under which “Mercury” had been living and, in a personal address to the suspect, said: "You are a coward, a slug and a murderer and you should do the right thing now explain that confession you made in 1971 and admit to your egregious crime," he said, adding, "You have not gotten away with anything."

News.title: Shifting stories and unanswered questions about Trump's war on Iran
News.content:
In the lead up to the 28 February strikes on Iran, President Donald Trump and his administration signalled that an attack was imminent.

Since then, the president and his top officials have offered varying explanations for its actions in the war with Iran.

The BBC's Analysis Editor Ros Atkins takes an in-depth look at how the Trump administration's narrative on the war with Iran has shifted, and the key questions that remain unanswered.

News.title: Satellite images show Iranian navy and nuclear sites hit
News.content: But he emphasised that Iran retains the ability to launch unconventional attacks at sea through the use of drones, mini-submarines and shadow fleet vessels - a network of tankers sailing under obscured ownership. MAIAR analysts also told BBC Verify that Tehran could turn to smaller, fast-attack vessels armed with anti-ship missiles in the coming days as Iran's largest warships continue to be targeted by US and Israeli strikes.
